posts.name: Mark Chatwin
posts.cooked:
Got this email from a vendor (Wine Express) and this taster (looks like a French magazine) who I never heard of describes a burgundy like this:

Domaine Guy & Yvan Dufouleur 2019 Hautes Cotes de Nuits Rouge, Les dames Huguettes

93 POINTS BY VERT DEVIN SAYING:
“The nose is elegant and offers a small richness, gourmandize as well as a certain / fine power. There are notes of ripe blackberry, blueberry and small notes of cassis associated with a touch of cherry tail, ripe / fresh red berries as well as hints of pepper, lilac, spices and a very discreet almost tonka bean / incense hint. The palate is fruity, well-balanced, slightly tight and offers juiciness, a small freshness, suavity, gourmandize, a beautiful pulpy / juicy fruit, generosity, roundness, a small fat as well as a fine straightness. On the palate this wine expresses notes of pulpy / fleshy cherry, pulpy / fleshy raspberry and small notes of purple plum associated with touches of cornflowers, blue berries as well as hints of cherry stone, licorice stick, zan and a subtle hint of sweet spices. Tannins are very discreetly firm. Presence of a subtle toasted hint on the finish / persistence. Good length.”

The words “gourmandize, certain/fine power, small richness, cherry tail, tonka bean, small freshness, small fat, and lastly good length” are tweaky.to me. There seems to be an attempt to be very detailed Like “cherry tail” and “zan” but bailouts like “good length” and the just plain bizarre “a small freshness”.

I guess in the end I find this review a bit on the wild side. It’s as if the reviewer is out of touch with reality.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:
“well-balanced, slightly tight”

I’m sure people will disagree with me, but I don’t think you can be both of these things at the same time. Tight implies young, and when wines are young, are often not totally balanced. Well-balanced to me says everything is in the right place.

I would be stunned if this wine tastes like 9/10ths of what they describe too. Cornflowers? Tonka Bean? Zan?

posts.name: Terry H a r r i s
posts.cooked: Detailed tasting notes are above my pay grade. I mostly just try to express whether I like, or really like, the wine and whether I think others will enjoy a similar experience. Occasionally I’ll mention a particular note I experience on the nose, but not much beyond that.
posts.display_username: Terry H a r r i s

posts.name: Marc Hauser
posts.cooked:
That is remarkable.

What does cornflower taste like?

And what is “zan”?

posts.name: Chuck Miller
posts.cooked:
The nuttiest note would mention hints of macadamia, almonds, peanut skins and cashews.

Sounds like a translation tool was used (French to English?).

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
FWIW, “tonka bean” is just a geeky alternative term for cinnamon. Both tonka beans and cinnamon contain the same chemical compound (coumarin) that gives cinnamon its distinctive aroma.

I’ve had some stuff flavored with tonka beans and usually they always have a very pronounced cinnamon flavor.

posts.name: Mark Chatwin
posts.cooked:
From my googling:

Zan
The most popular porridge recipe of monpa tribes of arunachal pradesh.It is prepared using millet flour.They have this as breakfast before going to work. It is nutritious and keeps you full.

Cherry Tail
The end piece of the cherry, i.e. - the stalk. Cherry tails have diuretic and antioxidant properties, they help prevent inflammation and fight muscle aches and joint pains. In alternative and traditional medicine, they are considered one of the top detoxifiers for the human body. Moreover, they has very good results in those who have problems with gallstones. Cherry stalks contain flavonoids, potassium salts and vitamins A, BA, B1, B2, B3, B6, C. Due to potassium, they activate the urinary tract.

Cornflower

The dried flowers are used to make medicine. People take cornflower tea to treat fever, constipation, water retention, and chest congestion. They also take it as a tonic, bitter, and liver and gallbladder stimulant. Women take it for menstrual disorders and vaginal yeast infections.

I may not like the wine reviewer’s take on wine but he/she is likely a very qualified holistic healer…

posts.name: Gavin.Curd
posts.cooked: I am the exact same way. Maybe I don’t have the palate that others have (to be honest I know I don’t), but specific flavor notes are not my forte. I can distinguish if a wine is acidic, fruity, well balanced, sweet, etc and I can tell if a wine is well balanced, but when it comes to very specific flavor notes I typically don’t differentiate a ton. I often utilize the same notes such as citrus, slate, fruity, floral, diesel, oak, spicy, etc. Much more generalized terms that I can understand and actually taste. I absolutely love reading notes like this and wish I could taste intricate flavors. Alas, I cannot at this point in my wine life.

Mark_Chatwin:

I find this review a bit on the wild side.

is this what it takes to bring you back? Welcome back.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: It’s just a bad translation / google translate of French. “Gourmandise” in French = sweetness/generosity. “Cherry tail” would be a bad translation of cherry pit/stalk. Each country has its own idioms…

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
It’s not half as bad in French (Vertdevin is a magazine I have never heard of - I Googled it up) - just a bad translation which comes from the English version of the website:

Le nez est élégant et offre une petite richesse, de la gourmandise ainsi qu’une certaine/fine puissance. On y retrouve des notes de mûre mûre, de myrtille et plus légèrement de cassis associées à une touche de queue de cerise, des petites baies rouges mûres/fraiches ainsi qu’à des pointes de poivre, de lilas, d’épices et à une très discrète pointe presque fève tonka/encens. La bouche est fruitée, équilibrée, finement serrée et offre de la jutosité, une petite fraicheur, de la suavité, de la gourmandise, un joli fruit pulpeux/juteux, de la générosité, de la rondeur, un petit gras ainsi qu’une fine droiture. En bouche ce vin exprime des notes de cerise pulpeuse/charnue, de framboise pulpeuse/charnue et plus légèrement de prune violette associées à des touches de bleuet, de petites baies bleues ainsi qu’à des pointes de noyau de cerise, de bâton de réglisse, de zan et à une subtile pointe d’épices douces. Les tannins sont très discrètement fermes. Présence d’une subtile pointe de toasté sur la fin de bouche/persistance. Bonne longueur.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Julian_Marshall:

It’s not half as bad in French (Vertdevin is a magazine I have never heard of - I Googled it up) - just a bad translation which comes from the English version of the website:

Le nez est élégant et offre une petite richesse, de la gourmandise ainsi qu’une certaine/fine puissance. On y retrouve des notes de mûre mûre, de myrtille et plus légèrement de cassis associées à une touche de queue de cerise, des petites baies rouges mûres/fraiches ainsi qu’à des pointes de poivre, de lilas, d’épices et à une très discrète pointe presque fève tonka/encens. La bouche est fruitée, équilibrée, finement serrée et offre de la jutosité, une petite fraicheur, de la suavité, de la gourmandise, un joli fruit pulpeux/juteux, de la générosité, de la rondeur, un petit gras ainsi qu’une fine droiture. En bouche ce vin exprime des notes de cerise pulpeuse/charnue, de framboise pulpeuse/charnue et plus légèrement de prune violette associées à des touches de bleuet, de petites baies bleues ainsi qu’à des pointes de noyau de cerise, de bâton de réglisse, de zan et à une subtile pointe d’épices douces. Les tannins sont très discrètement fermes. Présence d’une subtile pointe de toasté sur la fin de bouche/persistance. Bonne longueur.

This is exactly why I translate my own Finnish TNs into English instead of translating them directly with computer (unlike, say, some users in CT). Google Translate isn’t really built to understand and translate wine lingo, at least not yet.

Anyway, it was fun to see the original TN and how it made perfect sense in the original language. One could basically see the original note from the translated one just by reverse-engineering all the things that didn’t make sense.
Not to speak of the fact that most of these flavors and aromas are volatile - and will change even over a shorter period, much more over years-
